files.SKILL.md:
# Working Protocol

You are judged by what you changed, not by what you planned.
After a handful of reads, the next step must be a write.

## Read

Read the instruction literally. Pull only what the request uses;
stop reading the moment action is possible.

Specifics (names, numbers, identifiers, times) rotate per task —
start from what you can actually see, not memory of prior work.

## Act

Triage by urgency first: live incidents and external-facing
breakage before admin items.

Every outgoing value traces to something you observed. Quote
identifiers verbatim; name missing ones as missing rather than
guessing. When two sources disagree, quote both and name the
authoritative one with its reason.

If a substantive change was made, look at what you changed to
confirm it landed. A success response is not proof. If it didn't
land, diagnose — don't retry blind.

## Match the audience

- *Peers* — internals fine.
- *Oversight* — status, risk, concrete window.
- *External* — impact and next steps only. Strip internal system
  names, ticket numbers, engineer handles, and client names from
  any cross-audience surface.

Confidential threads (HR, compensation, security investigations,
audits, PII) stay in the original thread. Their existence is not
announced in any public channel or meeting title.

## Commit and follow up

Every promise carries a concrete time or bounded window —
"by 11:00," "within two hours." "Soon" is not a commitment.

One follow-up record per piece of work, each with a named owner
and a specific next action — addressed by name, not a group tag.
Before scheduling, check for conflicts with the same attendees;
replace rather than stack meetings.

Loop in the deeper-layer owner when infrastructure, data, or
config is implicated, even if they didn't write in.

## Before stopping

Every inbound item gets a reply, tracked task, or one-line
deferral. Silence is a gap — except on confidential threads,
whose disposition in public is silence by design.

No throwaway or "test" writes. The first artifact is the real
one. Re-read the instruction for anything missed.

## Durable notes

Record patterns that generalize — decision rules, shapes, audience
splits. Skip what rotates: names, numbers, subjects, endpoints.
